accountants.title: CPA & Accountant Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
accountants.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for CPAs and accountants. Protect your income against cognitive impairment, burnout from tax-season intensity, and mental health conditions. See how carriers differ on mental/nervous limitation clauses.
accountants.tldr: Accountants qualify for favorable 6A occupation class with most top carriers, making disability insurance cost-effective relative to income protected. Own-occupation coverage is critical because accounting demands sustained cognitive precision that neurological or psychological conditions can impair, even when physical capacity remains intact. Specialized guidance makes sure the right contract language for cognitive disability and mental health provisions, which represent your highest disability risk.

anesthesiologists.title: Disability Insurance for Anesthesiologists | Own-Occupation Coverage
anesthesiologists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for anesthesiologists. Protect your income against spinal disc disease from prolonged standing, needlestick exposure, and fine motor loss affecting intubation and regional techniques.
anesthesiologists.tldr: Anesthesiologists face high disability risk centered on spinal health and fine motor control despite favorable occupational class ratings. Own-occupation coverage is non-negotiable because back and neck injuries can prevent you from standing through cases even when you retain the ability to work in other medical roles, and the policy must protect your anesthesia-specific income. Specialized guidance addresses occupational hazards (needlestick exposure, substance sensitization) and makes sure your contract doesn't define you generically as a 'physician' where insurers could argue lower-paying medical work is available.

architects.title: Architect Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
architects.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for licensed architects. Protect your income against progressive vision loss, neurological conditions impairing spatial reasoning, and chronic pain from sustained design work.
architects.tldr: Most licensed architects qualify for favorable 5A or 6A occupation class ratings, keeping disability insurance premiums competitive relative to income protected. Own-occupation coverage is critical because architecture requires cognitive, visual, and creative capacity beyond general office work; visual impairment or neurological conditions can eliminate your ability to practice even if other work remains possible. Specialized guidance makes sure the right contract language and carrier match for your practice structure, whether you're a principal, independent practitioner, or firm employee.

attorneys.title: Attorney Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
attorneys.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for attorneys. A claim is decided by the definition type applied to your real duties, and most attorneys can secure full-period mental health coverage.
attorneys.tldr: For an attorney, the provisions that decide a claim are the own-occupation definition type and your residual coverage, not whether the contract names litigation or transactional work. A claim is measured against the material duties you were actually performing when disability began. Most attorneys are not in the high-risk group that faces a forced 24-month mental and nervous cap, so full-benefit-period mental health coverage is generally available across the carriers we place. Terms are set at underwriting, where about 28% of the policies in our placed book carry an exclusion or rating, so applying while healthy is the real lever.

business-finance.title: Business & Finance Disability Insurance
business-finance.description: Own-occupation disability coverage for executives, business owners, and financial professionals. Complex compensation structures require disciplined underwriting.

business-owners.title: Disability Insurance for Business Owners | Own-Occupation Coverage
business-owners.description: Business owners face two exposures: personal income that stops and business costs that do not. Compare individual coverage plus Business Overhead Expense, where Ameritas writes the highest limit.
business-owners.tldr: A business owner has two distinct exposures when disability hits: personal income that stops, and business operating costs that do not. Individual disability insurance protects the first; Business Overhead Expense, buy-sell, and key-person coverage protect the second. The right structure depends on how you take income, whether you have partners, and what the business owes. Of the carriers we place, Ameritas writes the highest BOE limit, $100,000 of monthly expense reimbursement versus roughly $50,000 at the others.

crna-quote-comparison.title: CRNA Disability Insurance: Carrier Comparison
crna-quote-comparison.description: How the five major disability carriers, Guardian, MassMutual, Principal, Ameritas, and The Standard, classify, underwrite, and cover CRNAs. Occupation class, own-occupation definitions, riders, and exclusions vary in ways that affect both premium and whether a claim pays.
crna-quote-comparison.tldr: Across the five major disability carriers, CRNA occupation class, own-occupation definitions, and rider structure vary enough that the same CRNA profile produces materially different policies and premiums. The right carrier generally depends on practice setting, health history, and which contract provisions matter most for anesthesia work. In Seaworthy's CRNA placements, MassMutual and Principal are selected most often, with the comparison run across all five carriers before any recommendation.

crnas.title: CRNA Disability Insurance
crnas.description: Seaworthy Insurance specializes in CRNA disability insurance. All five top carriers compared, with each carrier's own-occupation definition reviewed for anesthesia providers.
crnas.tldr: Seaworthy Insurance places CRNA coverage across all five major carriers, with every quote evaluated against occupation class, own-occupation contract language, rider structure, and premium.

data-scientists.title: Disability Insurance for Data Scientists & ML Engineers | Own-Occupation
data-scientists.description: Disability insurance for data scientists, data engineers, and ML engineers in the AI boom. Coverage that protects advanced quantitative and modeling work through a true own-occupation definition, sized to fast-rising AI-era compensation, with the top 6A occupation class and all five carriers compared.
data-scientists.tldr: Data scientists, data engineers, and ML engineers are at the center of the AI hiring surge, where pay is high and climbing fast. The field is newer, so many of these high earners are making a coverage decision for the first time, and two things deserve the most attention: a true own-occupation definition that protects advanced quantitative reasoning and statistical or machine-learning modeling rather than letting a carrier collapse it into generic computer work, and a benefit sized to AI-era total compensation. These roles also sit in the top 6A occupation class, which means strong contract terms and competitive pricing. We run all five major carriers on every case.

dental.title: Dental Professional Disability Insurance
dental.description: Own-occupation disability coverage for dentists and dental specialists. Fine motor demands and repetitive procedures require coverage that reflects your specific clinical role.

dentist-anesthesiologists.title: Dentist Anesthesiologist Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
dentist-anesthesiologists.description: Compare disability insurance for dentist anesthesiologists. Protect against hand tremor, cognitive impairment affecting sedation management, and carrier misclassification of your specialty. See which carriers properly underwrite dental anesthesia roles.
dentist-anesthesiologists.tldr: Dentist anesthesiologists are among the most underserved dental specialties in disability insurance underwriting. Carriers often misclassify you as either a general dentist or an anesthesiologist, missing the distinctive occupational structure of dental anesthesia: you combine anesthetic delivery with airway management in a dental setting, manage pediatric and special-needs patients under sedation, and carry malpractice exposure disproportionate to other dental specialties. Your policy must explicitly define dental anesthesia as the occupational base, not subordinate to either general dentistry or physician anesthesiology.

dentists.title: Disability Insurance for Dentists | Own-Occupation Coverage
dentists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for dentists. Protect against hand injury, carpal tunnel, and musculoskeletal strain from clinical positioning. See how carriers differ on ergonomic injury coverage and practice overhead protection.
dentists.tldr: Dentists qualify for favorable 5A or 6A occupational classes, making premiums competitive relative to income protected. Own-occupation coverage is critical because clinical dentistry depends on fine motor control, hand stability, and absence of tremor; any hand injury or musculoskeletal condition affecting your ability to perform procedures can terminate clinical practice. Specialized guidance on hand-injury provisions, ergonomic-strain language, and practice overhead coverage makes sure your contract matches the actual demands and risks of dental practice.

endodontists.title: Endodontist Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
endodontists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for endodontists. Protect against cervical disc disease from microscope positioning, carpal tunnel from file manipulation, and visual strain. See how carriers cover gradual loss of microsurgical precision.
endodontists.tldr: Endodontists qualify for favorable 5A or 6A occupation class ratings, but disability risk is concentrated in hand function, cervical spine health, and visual acuity; microsurgical precision within fractions of a millimeter is non-negotiable. Musculoskeletal conditions (cervical disc disease, carpal tunnel, de Quervain tendinopathy) are the leading disability pathways. Own-occupation definition must specifically protect endodontic practice, not reduce you to general dentistry income, and residual riders are critical given the likelihood of gradual decline rather than sudden total disability.

engineers.title: Engineer Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
engineers.description: Compare disability insurance quotes for engineers. Protect your income against cognitive impairment, TBI, and neurological conditions that threaten mathematical reasoning and systems analysis. See how carriers rate field vs. office engineering roles.
engineers.tldr: Engineers typically qualify for 5A or 6A occupational class ratings in office-based roles, with lower classifications for field-based specialties. Disability risk is centered on cognitive function; neurological conditions, traumatic brain injury, stroke, or cognitive effects of medical treatment can end engineering careers by impairing mathematical reasoning and systems-thinking capacity. Own-occupation coverage is essential because 'any occupation' definitions allow carriers to deny claims based on generic computer work, ignoring the difference between data entry and professional engineering analysis.

geriatricians.title: Geriatrician Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
geriatricians.description: Compare disability insurance quotes for geriatricians. Protect against compassion fatigue, infectious disease exposure in long-term care settings, and musculoskeletal strain from patient handling. See which carriers offer extended mental health benefits.
geriatricians.tldr: Geriatricians manage the most medically complex patients in medicine while bearing the psychological weight of end-of-life care and compassion fatigue. Mental and nervous limitation clauses capping psychiatric benefits at 24 months are inadequate for a specialty where burnout is endemic rather than exceptional. Disability risk spans cognitive (multi-system disease management), physical (patient handling), and psychological (moral distress) domains. Own-occupation coverage and extended mental health provisions are essential; carrier selection is critical for this specialty.

infectious-disease.title: Infectious Disease Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
infectious-disease.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for infectious disease physicians. Protect your income against bloodborne pathogen exposure, needle stick injuries, and burnout from managing critically ill patients. See how carriers handle occupational infection risk.
infectious-disease.tldr: Infectious disease specialists qualify for favorable occupational classifications, but own-occupation coverage is essential because your income depends entirely on cognitive diagnostic capacity. Pathogen exposure, burnout from managing critically ill patients, and occupational psychological stress create disability pathways that generic policies fail to address adequately.

legal.title: Attorney & Legal Professional Disability Insurance
legal.description: Own-occupation disability coverage for attorneys, judges, and legal professionals. Cognitive disability risk and complex income structures require specialized underwriting.

nephrologists.title: Nephrologist Disability Insurance: Compare Top Carriers
nephrologists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for nephrologists. Coverage built for burnout, dialysis exposure, and procedural risk.
nephrologists.tldr: Nephrologists need own-occupation coverage that protects against burnout and psychiatric disability from managing chronically ill patients with high mortality rates. Mental health provisions are critical; policies capping psychological disability at 24 months are inadequate for a specialty where burnout is a documented occupational pathway. Infectious disease exposure in dialysis environments adds procedural disability risk.

obgyn.title: Disability Insurance for OB/GYN | Own-Occupation Coverage
obgyn.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for OB/GYNs. Protect your income against back injury from delivery positioning, burnout from unpredictable call, and malpractice-related psychological disability. See how carriers treat obstetric vs. gynecologic practice mix.
obgyn.tldr: OB/GYNs require own-occupation coverage that protects the full hybrid practice, not obstetrics or gynecology separately. Unpredictable on-call scheduling creates cumulative burnout and sleep deprivation that standard definitions underestimate. Back injuries from delivery positioning and cesarean work are the most common disability trigger; policies must cover occupational back and neck injury without arbitrary benefit period limits.

optometrists.title: Disability Insurance for Optometrists | Own-Occupation Coverage
optometrists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for optometrists. Protect your income against vision loss, hand and wrist repetitive strain, and eye strain from prolonged slit lamp use. See how carriers cover the visual acuity demands unique to ODs.
optometrists.tldr: Optometrists need own-occupation coverage that explicitly covers vision loss and visual impairment as disability pathways, not as excluded conditions. Your ability to examine patients and interpret diagnostic findings depends entirely on your own visual function; policies must protect against the profession-specific risk that other healthcare professionals don't face.

oral-surgeons.title: Disability Insurance for Oral Surgeons | Own-Occupation Coverage
oral-surgeons.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for oral surgeons. Protect your surgical income against cervical spine strain, hand injury from forceful extractions, and needle stick exposure. See how carriers classify OMS versus general dentistry.
oral-surgeons.tldr: Oral surgeons require own-occupation coverage that reflects the surgical scope of their practice, not general dentistry, and protects the substantial income difference. Cervical strain from confined operative field positioning and hand injury from forceful surgical movements are predictable occupational consequences; policies must cover these without imposing arbitrary benefit limits.

orthodontists.title: Orthodontist Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
orthodontists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for orthodontists. Protect your income against carpal tunnel from high-volume wire adjustments, cervical disc disease, and eye strain from magnification. Get residual disability coverage for reduced patient throughput.
orthodontists.tldr: Orthodontists face significant cumulative musculoskeletal risk from high-volume repetitive practice despite favorable occupational classifications. Own-occupation coverage with residual disability riders is essential because partial disability from carpal tunnel, cervical disc disease, or hand conditions directly impacts income from reduced patient throughput, not elimination of the profession entirely.

pediatric-dentists.title: Pediatric Dentist Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
pediatric-dentists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for pediatric dentists. Protect your income against extreme ergonomic strain from small-patient positioning, carpal tunnel from confined operative fields, and physical injury from behavior management. See how carriers classify pediatric dental risk.
pediatric-dentists.tldr: Pediatric dentists face extreme ergonomic strain from working with small patients in compromised positions for extended periods, combined with physical demands of behavior management and sustained fine motor precision. Carpal tunnel, back injury, neck strain, and hand loss of dexterity are occupational inevitabilities. Own-occupation coverage secures full benefits when musculoskeletal disability prevents the precision dentistry work that generates your $280K+ income.

periodontists.title: Periodontist Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
periodontists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for periodontists. Protect your surgical income against carpal tunnel from implant placement, cervical disc disease from sustained positioning, and hand tremor affecting microsurgical precision. Get residual coverage for reduced caseloads.
periodontists.tldr: Periodontists perform high-precision surgical techniques requiring sustained hand dexterity, fine motor control, and extreme ergonomic positioning. Carpal tunnel, hand tremor, back and neck injury from prolonged positioning, and repetitive strain affecting surgical precision are career-ending injuries. Own-occupation coverage secures full benefits when hand loss or musculoskeletal disability prevents the surgical periodontology that generates your $300K+ income.

pharmacists.title: Pharmacist Disability Insurance | PharmD Quote Comparison
pharmacists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for pharmacists. Protect your income against cognitive decline affecting drug interaction verification, lower back pain from prolonged standing, and repetitive strain from high-volume dispensing. See how carriers classify PharmD roles.
pharmacists.tldr: Pharmacists depend on cognitive function and specialized pharmaceutical knowledge that cannot be replaced in non-pharmacy roles. Cognitive decline, memory loss, psychiatric disability preventing decision-making, or burnout that compromises your ability to verify prescriptions safely is career-ending. Own-occupation coverage secures full benefits when cognitive or psychiatric disability prevents the clinical pharmacist work that generates your income.

physician-assistants.title: Physician Assistant Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
physician-assistants.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for physician assistants. Protect your procedural and clinical income against needle stick exposure, musculoskeletal injury, and burnout. See how carriers classify PAs by specialty setting.
physician-assistants.tldr: PAs perform clinical and procedural work comparable to physicians within their specialty, but generic disability policies often undervalue your role by defining disability around physician income or supervisory functions rather than your actual clinical duties. Own-occupation coverage secures full benefits when disability prevents your specific clinical and procedural scope of practice, not your employer's supervisory requirements.

physicians.title: Physician & Surgeon Disability Insurance
physicians.description: Own-occupation disability coverage for 50+ physician and surgeon specialties. Each specialty faces distinct occupational risk requiring precision underwriting.

product-managers.title: Disability Insurance for Product Managers | Own-Occupation for a Judgment Role
product-managers.description: Disability insurance for tech product managers. The central issue is the own-occupation definition: a role defined by judgment and decision-making is harder to pin down at claim time, so a true own-occupation contract that measures disability against the actual PM job is what protects the income. Top 6A class, all five carriers compared.
product-managers.tldr: A product manager's value is judgment, strategy, and cross-functional leadership, not a measurable physical or coding output. That makes the own-occupation definition the decision that matters most: a role defined by decision-making is harder to pin down at claim time, so a true own-occupation contract that measures disability against the actual PM job is what keeps the income protected. Product managers also sit in a top occupation class (6A), and an individual policy stays portable across the job and startup moves a product career runs on. We compare all five major carriers on contract language and price.

professionals.title: Professional Disability Insurance
professionals.description: Own-occupation disability coverage for allied health professionals, tech workers, engineers, pilots, and other high-income professionals.

prosthodontists.title: Prosthodontist Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
prosthodontists.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for prosthodontists. Protect your income against carpal tunnel from precision crown work, cervical disc disease from extended procedures, and hand tremor that compromises margin accuracy in implant restorations.
prosthodontists.tldr: Prosthodontists require exceptional fine motor precision for complex prosthodontic procedures including implant prosthetics, precision removable prosthetics, and complex laboratory coordination. Carpal tunnel, hand tremor, arthritis affecting fine motor dexterity, and back strain from sustained positioning is career-ending. Own-occupation coverage secures full benefits when hand or musculoskeletal disability prevents the specialized prosthodontic work generating your $280K+ income.

residents.title: Disability Insurance for Medical Residents and Fellows
residents.description: Why residents and fellows should lock in individual disability coverage during training: clean-record underwriting, a Future Increase Option, resident discounts, and the gaps in GME group plans.
residents.tldr: Training is the right time to put an individual disability policy in place, but not for the reason most marketing claims. The durable advantages are health and age (you are rated on a clean record before income rises), a Future Increase Option that locks insurability before any health or exclusion event, and portability the GME group plan does not give you. Real resident discounts exist (MassMutual's 20% medical-resident and 10% dental-resident programs, plus general AMA and institutional programs), but the bigger lever is applying before any condition lands on your chart.

software-engineers.title: Disability Insurance for Software Engineers: RSI & Own-Occupation
software-engineers.description: Software engineers are the one tech role where hands-on injury is a real claim driver. Own-occupation coverage that protects against repetitive strain, carpal tunnel, and tendinopathy, written so a carrier cannot deny a claim just because you can still type. Class 6A pricing, all five carriers compared.
software-engineers.tldr: Software engineers are the one tech role where physical, hands-on risk is a genuine claim driver: years of sustained keyboard and mouse use commonly lead to repetitive strain injury, carpal tunnel, and tendinopathy in the exact hands the job runs on. That makes the own-occupation definition the decision that matters most. An any-occupation contract lets a carrier argue that an engineer who can still type is not disabled, which ignores the gap between basic computer use and professional engineering. Engineers sit in occupation class 6A and are usually straightforward to place well; we run all five major carriers on every case.

software-sales.title: Disability Insurance for Tech Sales | Coverage Sized to OTE
software-sales.description: Disability insurance for software and tech sales professionals: account executives, sales engineers, and account managers. Why group coverage insures base only and misses most of your on-target earnings, how carriers count and average commission, and why portability matters when you change companies often. All five carriers compared.
software-sales.tldr: Tech sales pay is mostly variable: base salary plus commission against an on-target-earnings (OTE) number, often with commission running half or more of the total. The problem that defines this role is that group long-term disability insures base salary only, so it protects a fraction of what a rep actually earns. An individual policy can be sized to total OTE because commission is earned income, which carriers count and average over about two years. It also stays with you through the frequent job changes a sales career brings. We run all five major carriers on every case.

sports-medicine.title: Sports Medicine Disability Insurance | Own-Occupation Coverage
sports-medicine.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance for sports medicine physicians. Protect your income against repetitive strain from ultrasound-guided procedures, musculoskeletal injury from high-volume exams, and sideline trauma exposure.
sports-medicine.tldr: Sports medicine physicians face high-volume musculoskeletal examination demands plus sideline coverage exposure. Own-occupation coverage secures protection if shoulder, wrist, or ankle injury; occupational arthritis; or overuse syndrome prevents ultrasound-guided procedures, even if you could do non-procedural sports medicine or telemedicine work.

surgeons.title: Disability Insurance for Surgeons | Own-Occupation Coverage
surgeons.description: Compare own-occupation disability insurance quotes for surgeons. Protect your income against hand tremor, cervical disc disease from OR posture, and needle stick exposure. See how carriers define disability for operative vs. non-operative work.
surgeons.tldr: Surgeons require true own-occupation coverage that protects against hand tremor, vision loss, and back injury that prevent operative work, even though you could theoretically do consultative or non-operative work. Own-occupation definition makes sure you're covered for inability to operate, not just inability to practice medicine.

tech.title: Tech Disability Insurance
tech.description: Seaworthy Insurance places disability insurance for software engineers, developers, tech sales, product managers, and data professionals. All five top carriers compared on occupation class, own-occupation language, mental-health treatment, and price.
tech.tldr: Seaworthy Insurance places tech coverage across all five major carriers, with every quote evaluated against occupation class, own-occupation contract language, how the benefit is sized to equity-heavy pay, and premium.

aana-disability-plan.title: The AANA Disability Plan for CRNAs: What It Covers and Where It Falls Short
aana-disability-plan.description: The AANA Member Advantage disability plan from New York Life: what it covers, the $12,000 cap, the 12-month mental-health limit, age-banded premiums, and where a high-earning CRNA needs an individual policy instead.
aana-disability-plan.tldr: The AANA Member Advantage plan is a member-paid group disability plan from New York Life. Premiums are age-banded and rise as you age, where an individual non-cancelable policy locks the rate to 65. It caps benefits at $12,000 a month (and at 70% of income, under a $20,000 aggregate), pays residual only after a prior period of total disability, limits mental/nervous and substance claims to 12 months, and reduces income earned in another occupation by 50%. Coverage is tied to AANA membership and ends by age 70. For a high-earning CRNA, an individual true own-occupation policy carries a higher benefit, residual on income loss alone, a longer mental/nervous benefit, and a premium fixed for the life of the contract.

crna-disability-insurance-by-career-stag.title: CRNA Disability Insurance by Career Stage: New Graduate to Retirement
crna-disability-insurance-by-career-stag.description: How the right disability coverage decision changes for a CRNA across career stages: locking in early as a new graduate, closing the underinsurance gap mid-career, and matching coverage to the retirement timeline.
crna-disability-insurance-by-career-stag.tldr: The right disability decision for a CRNA changes with career stage. New graduates should lock in coverage during the clean-underwriting window and use future increase options to scale it. Mid-career CRNAs need to close the gap between a policy sized to a starting salary and current income, before accumulated wear is documented and complicates re-underwriting. CRNAs approaching retirement should match the benefit period to their planned retirement age and watch for an occupation-definition mismatch if they move into non-clinical work.

crna-disability-insurance-mistakes.title: 7 Disability Insurance Mistakes CRNAs Make Before Mid-Career
crna-disability-insurance-mistakes.description: The common gaps in CRNA disability coverage before they become claims: relying on group LTD alone, an any-occupation definition, skipped riders, inflation exposure, and bad timing. What each costs and how to fix it.
crna-disability-insurance-mistakes.tldr: Common CRNA coverage gaps include relying on hospital group coverage alone, accepting an any-occupation definition, skipping the residual rider that protects partial-disability claims, and leaving off the cost-of-living rider on a long claim. Each leaves a high earner under-protected, and nearly all of them are settled at application, long before a claim is ever filed.

crna-disability-risks.title: CRNA Disability Risks and How Disability Coverage Responds
crna-disability-risks.description: Seaworthy Insurance specializes in CRNA coverage. The disability risks CRNAs face most, from musculoskeletal injury to mental health and substance use, and how the policy definition and limitations decide each claim.
crna-disability-risks.tldr: The disability risks most likely to interrupt or end a CRNA's career fall into four categories: musculoskeletal injury, mental health and burnout, substance use disorder, and hand or nerve conditions. Whether a claim pays turns on a few policy-level details: a true own-occupation definition paired with being classified as a nurse anesthetist, a benefit period long enough for multi-year conditions, and the mental and nervous limitation that commonly caps psychiatric claims at 24 months.

crna-group-vs-individual.title: CRNA Group vs Individual Disability Insurance: Why Hospital Coverage Falls Short
crna-group-vs-individual.description: CRNA group LTD plans commonly cap benefits around $10,000/mo, pay taxable benefits, provide only time-limited own-occupation protection, and end at job change. Individual coverage closes the after-tax gap.
crna-group-vs-individual.tldr: Hospital group LTD plans for CRNAs commonly cap benefits around $10,000 per month, and because employer-paid group benefits are generally taxable, a stated $10,000 benefit typically nets closer to $6,500 after tax. An individual policy is paid tax-free and can be written as true own-occupation, so the after-tax benefit an individual policy can add is often several thousand dollars per month even when the face-value figures look close.

crna-own-occupation.title: How Own-Occupation Definitions Affect CRNA Disability Claims
crna-own-occupation.description: Seaworthy Insurance specializes in CRNA own-occupation coverage. A CRNA's claim is decided by a true own-occupation definition applied to anesthesia work; correct classification and documented income at underwriting set the occupation class, premium, and benefit size.
crna-own-occupation.tldr: Own-occupation is the provision that decides a CRNA disability claim. The major carriers do not name anesthesia in the contract; each measures disability against the material and substantial duties of your occupation at the time disability begins, which for a working CRNA is the delivery of anesthesia. A true own-occupation definition keeps paying total-disability benefits even if you can no longer give anesthesia but work in another occupation. Being underwritten and classified as a nurse anesthetist, with full CRNA income documented, still matters, but it sets your occupation class, premium, and benefit size, not the standard the claim is judged by. The main risk to watch is an any-occupation definition, settled before you ever file a claim.

crna-practice-settings.title: CRNA Practice Settings and How Each Shapes Disability Coverage
crna-practice-settings.description: Seaworthy Insurance specializes in CRNA coverage. Hospital, anesthesia group, independent contractor, and locum tenens work each drive different income documentation and policy provision requirements.
crna-practice-settings.tldr: Where and how you practice anesthesia directly shapes what a disability policy needs to cover. Hospital-employed CRNAs, anesthesia group members, independent contractors, and locum tenens providers each face different income documentation realities, occupational definition considerations, and portability concerns. Structuring coverage around the actual practice setting prevents gaps that surface only at claim time.

crna-questions-before-buying.title: 8 Questions Every CRNA Should Ask Before Buying Disability Insurance
crna-questions-before-buying.description: Seaworthy Insurance outlines eight questions CRNAs should ask before buying disability coverage, from the own-occupation definition to occupation class, residual activation, and underwriting, so you know how a policy pays before you need it.
crna-questions-before-buying.tldr: The premium and the monthly benefit are the easy parts of a CRNA disability policy. What decides whether a claim pays is the definition of disability, the occupation class, the riders, and how the policy was underwritten. These eight questions cover each of those before you buy, while you can still change the answer.

tech-disability-insurance-cost.title: How Much Disability Insurance Costs for Tech Workers
tech-disability-insurance-cost.description: What individual disability insurance costs for technology professionals and what drives the premium. Office-based tech sits in occupation class 6A, a top class that prices well. Age, health, benefit amount, elimination period, benefit period, and riders all move the price. Industry premiums commonly run roughly 1% to 3% of income.
tech-disability-insurance-cost.tldr: Tech is inexpensive to insure relative to the income it protects, because office-based technology roles sit in occupation class 6A, one of the top occupation classes carriers use. Low-hazard cognitive work prices well. As a general industry guideline, individual disability premiums commonly run roughly 1% to 3% of income, and the figure tracks age and health at application, occupation class, benefit amount, elimination period, benefit period, and rider selection. Buying early, while young and healthy, locks in the lowest rate and the best health class.

tech-group-vs-individual.title: Group vs. Individual Disability Insurance for Tech Workers
tech-group-vs-individual.description: Why employer group LTD underinsures tech professionals: it generally covers base salary only, caps the monthly benefit, pays a taxable benefit, switches to any-occupation after about 24 months, and ends when you leave. How an individual own-occupation policy fills the gap for equity- and bonus-heavy pay.
tech-group-vs-individual.tldr: Employer group long-term disability is the default coverage a tech worker has, and it has structural limits: it generally replaces about 60% of base salary only, commonly caps the monthly benefit around $10,000 for most employees, pays a taxable benefit when the employer funds the premium, applies an own-occupation test that commonly lasts about 24 months before switching to an any-occupation standard, and ends the day you leave the job. Because tech pay is weighted toward bonus and equity, a base-only group benefit protects a fraction of total compensation. An individual own-occupation policy is the fix: it pays its stated benefit regardless of other coverage, can be sized to total earned income, is portable across job changes, and locks in your rate and health class. Most tech workers keep both, with the individual policy carrying the core weight.

tech-h1b-visa.title: Disability Insurance for H-1B & Visa-Holding Tech Workers
tech-h1b-visa.description: Why individual disability insurance matters for technology workers on H-1B and other work visas. Group coverage ends when employment ends, which for a visa holder can coincide with a status problem. How carriers generally underwrite applicants on temporary work visas, how to size the benefit to total earned income, and why timing while employed and healthy matters.
tech-h1b-visa.tldr: For a technology worker on an H-1B or similar work visa, income protection carries a risk most US-born workers do not face: work-visa status is generally tied to employment, so a disability that ends your job can also threaten your status. An individual disability policy is owned by you and stays in force regardless of employer or status changes, while group long-term disability ends the day employment ends. All five major carriers we work with will consider applicants on H-1B and similar work visas, generally case-by-case, and green-card holders are typically underwritten on the same basis as US citizens. Terms differ by carrier, so running all five finds the best fit. Buying while employed, healthy, and authorized to work locks in coverage and rate, with technology occupations generally classified 6A, one of the most favorable classes.

tech-mental-health.title: Mental-Health & Burnout Disability Coverage for Tech Workers
tech-mental-health.description: Why most disability contracts cap mental and nervous claims at 24 months, why tech professionals are usually exempt from that limitation, and how applying before any treatment is on record keeps full-benefit-period mental-health coverage available.
tech-mental-health.tldr: Burnout, anxiety, and depression are among the most common reasons a tech professional would file a disability claim, yet most contracts cap mental and nervous claims at 24 months even when the policy otherwise pays to age 65. Tech professionals are usually exempt from that cap, so full-benefit-period mental-health coverage is available to them in a way it is not for some higher-risk medical specialties. It is available across the major carriers, by different mechanisms: by default at Guardian and Principal, as the standard option at Ameritas and The Standard, where the 24-month cap is only an optional discount, and by endorsement at MassMutual. The catch is timing: this coverage is only available before any mental-health treatment is on the record, which is the single strongest reason for a tech worker to apply early.

tech-own-occupation.title: Own-Occupation Disability Insurance for Tech Workers
tech-own-occupation.description: The own-occupation definition is the single provision that decides whether a tech professional's claim pays. The three definition types explained, the 'you can still use a computer' trap, and why the definition you bought and the duties of your occupation at the time of disability, not contract keywords, decide a claim.
tech-own-occupation.tldr: The own-occupation definition is the provision that decides whether a tech worker's disability claim pays. A true own-occupation contract pays if you cannot perform the material and substantial duties of your own occupation, even if you go on to work and earn in a different field. A modified or transitional definition stops paying once you work elsewhere, and an any-occupation definition pays only if you cannot work at all. What actually decides a claim is two things together: the definition type you bought, and the duties of the occupation you were actually performing when disability begins. Tech roles generally qualify for class 6A, one of the most favorable occupation classes, where true own-occupation coverage is available across the major carriers.

tech-rsu-equity-compensation.title: Disability Insurance for RSUs & Equity Compensation
tech-rsu-equity-compensation.description: How disability carriers treat RSUs, bonus, ESPP, and stock options when sizing a tech worker's benefit. Vested RSUs are W-2 wages and generally count when documented; unvested grants and options do not. What to document and how to size coverage to total compensation.
tech-rsu-equity-compensation.tldr: Disability carriers size a benefit to your earned income. Base salary, bonus, and commission are counted directly, with bonus and commission usually averaged over about two years. Vested RSUs are reported as W-2 wages, so that income generally counts when you can document a consistent vesting history. Unvested RSUs, unexercised stock options, and passive investment income are not counted. The practical goal for an equity-heavy tech worker is a benefit sized to total earned compensation, not just base salary, which is what most employer group coverage misses.

tech-startup-equity.title: Disability Insurance for Startup & Pre-IPO Tech Employees
tech-startup-equity.description: Startups often offer little or no group long-term disability, and your equity is illiquid until it produces income. How carriers underwrite a startup employee's salary today, why options and pre-IPO shares are not counted, and how to use a future increase option to grow the benefit after a liquidity event.
tech-startup-equity.tldr: Most startups offer little or no group long-term disability, so an early-stage employee is often almost entirely uninsured for income protection by default. Carriers underwrite earned income that has already been received and documented, which generally means your cash salary today. Stock options and pre-IPO shares are not counted until they produce W-2 income. The practical move is to buy an individual policy early while you are young, healthy, and cheap to insure, then use a future increase option to raise the benefit later as your income grows or your equity becomes liquid, with no new medical underwriting.

any-occupation-vs-own-occupation.title: Any-Occupation vs. Own-Occupation Disability Insurance
any-occupation-vs-own-occupation.description: Own-occupation pays if you cannot do your own job, even if you can work elsewhere. Any-occupation pays only if you cannot work at all. Why the distinction decides claims for high-income professionals, and where each definition shows up.
any-occupation-vs-own-occupation.tldr: Own-occupation covers you if you cannot perform your specific occupation, even if you work and earn in another field. Any-occupation covers you only if you cannot work in any occupation you're reasonably suited for. The gap matters most for professionals with transferable skills, and group plans almost always land on the weaker side of it.

benefit-period.title: Benefit Period Explained: How Long Disability Insurance Pays
benefit-period.description: The benefit period sets how long a disability policy pays. To-age-65 is the dominant structure in our placed book. Learn the trade-offs and the mental/nervous cap.
benefit-period.tldr: The benefit period is the maximum length of time a policy pays a claim. To-age-65 is the dominant choice in our placed book, paired with a 90-day elimination period. Shorter periods cost less but leave a gap if a disability outlasts them, and a separate mental/nervous limitation caps psychiatric claims at 24 months only for a defined high-risk occupation group.

carrier-specific-advantages.title: Carrier-Specific Advantages: How the Five Majors Actually Differ
carrier-specific-advantages.description: Guardian, MassMutual, Principal, Ameritas, and The Standard each have real strengths. Here is how we compare all five on contract language and fit, not price.
carrier-specific-advantages.tldr: The five major disability carriers each have a genuine, distinct strength: Guardian's contract and claims reputation, MassMutual's dividends and military and resident discounts, Principal's deep rider menu and flexible underwriting, Ameritas's high business-overhead limit, and The Standard's contract clarity and Family Care Benefit. We compare all five on contract language and fit for your occupation, not on price alone.

catastrophic-disability-rider.title: Catastrophic Disability Rider: When the Extra Layer Is Worth It
catastrophic-disability-rider.description: A catastrophic disability rider adds benefit for severe impairment (loss of 2+ ADLs). It is rare in practice and the cost-to-value math is usually unfavorable.
catastrophic-disability-rider.tldr: A catastrophic disability rider adds benefit on top of your base coverage when a disability is severe enough that you cannot perform two or more activities of daily living. The protection is real, but catastrophic disabilities are uncommon, and the cost-to-expected-value math is usually unfavorable. In our placed book (2025 audit), only about 3% of policies carry this rider; it is a situational add-on, not a core one.

cola-rider.title: Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA) Rider: Protecting Benefits Against Inflation
cola-rider.description: A COLA rider raises your disability benefit during a long claim so inflation does not erode it. See per-carrier structures and why most policies lack one.
cola-rider.tldr: A COLA rider increases your monthly benefit during a long claim so inflation does not quietly erode its value. It matters most for younger buyers with long benefit periods and loses value once you are past roughly age 50. Most policies we place do not carry one (COLA adoption is about 38% in our 2025 book audit), which leaves an inflation gap on the majority of long-term claims.

disability-insurance-claims-process.title: How a Disability Insurance Claim Actually Works
disability-insurance-claims-process.description: Total disability pays the fixed benefit your policy was issued for. The carrier verifies income at underwriting, not at claim. Here is how a claim really works.
disability-insurance-claims-process.tldr: A total-disability claim pays the fixed monthly benefit your policy was issued for. The carrier does not recalculate your pre-disability income at claim time, because income is verified at underwriting. Residual claims use your prior earnings, and passive income such as investments, dividends, and rent is excluded from the calculation entirely.

disability-insurance-cost.title: How Much Does Disability Insurance Cost? What High Earners Actually Pay
disability-insurance-cost.description: Individual disability insurance typically runs about 1-3% of annual income. Learn the factors that set the premium and why buying earlier costs less.
disability-insurance-cost.tldr: Individual disability insurance typically costs about 1 to 3 percent of annual income, but that range is wide because several factors set the price. Age and health are the largest levers, and both only move one direction over time, so applying earlier almost always costs less. Cost should follow the coverage decision, not lead it.

disability-insurance-for-locum-tenens.title: Disability Insurance for Locum Tenens and 1099 Physicians
disability-insurance-for-locum-tenens.description: How locum tenens and 1099 physicians document variable income for underwriting, secure true own-occupation coverage, and own a portable policy with no employer plan to fall back on.
disability-insurance-for-locum-tenens.tldr: Locum tenens and other 1099 physicians have no employer group plan and variable, multi-source income, which makes individual coverage both essential and a documentation exercise. Carriers underwrite earned income using a few years of tax returns and 1099s or W-2s, commonly averaging across years, and they exclude passive income. The two features that matter most are a true own-occupation definition and the portability of an individual policy, which travels with you across assignments and structures.

disability-insurance-myths-high-earners.title: Disability Insurance Myths That Cost High Earners Money
disability-insurance-myths-high-earners.description: Three myths cost high earners the most: that group coverage is enough, that a policy replaces a flat 60% of income, and that being healthy means you can wait. Here is what the data shows.
disability-insurance-myths-high-earners.tldr: The myths that cost high earners the most are that employer group coverage is enough, that a disability policy replaces a flat 60% of income, and that being healthy now means you can safely wait. Group LTD caps around $10,000, covers base only, and is taxable; individual benefits are income-based and the replacement ratio declines with income, leaving high earners replacing roughly a third of their pay; and waiting is the riskiest myth, because about 28% of the policies we place come back with an exclusion or rating tied to a documented condition.

disability-insurance-riders-explained.title: Disability Insurance Riders Explained: What Each One Does and Whether It's Worth the Premium
disability-insurance-riders-explained.description: What each disability insurance rider does, whether it earns its premium for a high earner, and which ones our clients actually adopt. Grounded in our placed-book data.
disability-insurance-riders-explained.tldr: Riders turn a base disability policy into coverage that fits how you actually earn, but they are not equal value. Residual is the foundation and almost always earns its cost, because most claims are partial rather than total. A COLA rider is high value under about 45; a future increase option is essential early in a career; return of premium, catastrophic, and social insurance supplement riders rarely clear the bar for high earners. Adoption in our placed book reflects this: roughly 38% carry COLA, about 30% carry a future increase option, and only about 3% carry a catastrophic rider.

disability-insurance-tax-guide.title: Disability Insurance and Taxes: What High Earners Need to Know
disability-insurance-tax-guide.description: IRS Pub 525: employer-paid group premiums make benefits taxable; after-tax individual premiums make them tax-free. See the net-of-tax math for high earners.
disability-insurance-tax-guide.tldr: The IRS rule is simple: who pays the premium decides whether the benefit is taxed. Per Publication 525, employer-paid group premiums make the benefit taxable, while after-tax individual premiums make it tax-free. For a high earner, a taxable benefit loses roughly 30 to 40 percent to combined federal and state tax, so a $10,000 group benefit nets about $6,000 to $7,000. Structuring premiums so the benefit is tax-free is one of the highest-value decisions in a disability plan.

elimination-period.title: Elimination Period Explained: The Waiting Period Before Benefits Begin
elimination-period.description: The elimination period is the wait before benefits start. 90 days is the standard in our placed book. Compare 60, 90, and 180 days and how each affects premium.
elimination-period.tldr: The elimination period is the number of days you must be disabled before benefits begin, like a deductible measured in time. A 90-day period is the standard in our placed book, paired with a to-age-65 benefit period. A shorter wait costs more; a longer wait costs less but means self-insuring for longer.

exclusion-riders.title: Exclusion Riders: How Common They Are and How We Get Them Removed
exclusion-riders.description: About 28% of the policies we place carry an exclusion or rating. Here is what they are, which professions see the most, and how exclusions get removed.
exclusion-riders.tldr: An exclusion rider carves a specific condition out of a disability policy in exchange for issuing coverage rather than declining it. In our placed book (2025 audit), roughly 28% of policies carry an exclusion or a rating, with mental and nervous history the single most common cause. An exclusion may be permanent, but in our experience it often is not: we negotiate for ratings and exclusions to be reconsiderable, and we have strong success getting them removed, commonly about two years after issue once a clean interval passes.

future-increase-options.title: Future Increase Option (FIO): Locking In Insurability Before It Costs You
future-increase-options.description: FIO lets you raise your disability benefit later without new medical underwriting, locking insurability before a health event or exclusion can limit you. Why it matters early.
future-increase-options.tldr: The future increase option lets you raise your disability benefit later without new medical underwriting. Its real job is locking in insurability before a health event, an exclusion, or a rating can limit you. That matters because roughly 28% of policies in our placed book come back with an exclusion or rating; once your health changes, new coverage can be limited or priced up. FIO is most valuable early in a career, yet only about 30% of policies in our book carry one.

graded-vs-level-premium.title: Graded vs. Level Premium Disability Insurance: How to Choose
graded-vs-level-premium.description: Level premium is the standard for career buyers; graded eases early-career cash flow but costs more long-term. How to choose by career stage, with median age at issue 36.
graded-vs-level-premium.tldr: A level premium stays flat for the life of the policy and is the standard structure for career buyers who intend to hold coverage to retirement. A graded premium starts lower and rises for the first several years, easing early-career cash flow but costing more over a long hold. With a median age at issue of 36 across our book, most buyers have a long enough horizon that level is the default.

group-vs-individual.title: Group vs. Individual Disability Insurance: Why High Earners Need Both
group-vs-individual.description: Individual own-occupation coverage is indemnity: it pays regardless of other income. Group LTD coordinates, caps, and is taxable. See why high earners need both.
group-vs-individual.tldr: The core difference is structural. An individual own-occupation policy is indemnity, so it pays its stated monthly benefit regardless of any other coverage. Group LTD coordinates with other income, caps the benefit (commonly around $10,000), covers base salary only, and is usually taxable when the employer pays. For a high earner, group alone tends to replace only about a third of income, which is the gap individual coverage is built to close.

guaranteed-renewable-vs-noncancelable.title: Guaranteed Renewable vs. Noncancelable Disability Insurance: The Two Guarantees
guaranteed-renewable-vs-noncancelable.description: Two guarantees control a disability policy. Noncancelable locks premium and terms; guaranteed renewable locks terms but lets premiums rise. Noncancelable is the professional standard the five majors offer.
guaranteed-renewable-vs-noncancelable.tldr: Disability policies carry two renewal guarantees. Guaranteed renewable locks your benefits and terms but lets the carrier raise premiums by class. Noncancelable locks both the premium and the terms to a stated age, usually 65. Noncancelable plus guaranteed renewable is the standard structure for professionals, and all five major carriers we place write it.

how-much-disability-insurance-do-i-need.title: How Much Disability Insurance Do You Need? Sizing Coverage for High Earners
how-much-disability-insurance-do-i-need.description: The benefit is income-based and the replacement ratio falls as income rises. For high earners the maximum often replaces about a third of income.
how-much-disability-insurance-do-i-need.tldr: Disability insurance is not a flat 60 percent of income. The maximum benefit a carrier will issue is income-based and the replacement ratio declines as income rises. For high earners the maximum often replaces only about a third of income, which is the coverage gap most professionals discover too late. Size your benefit to the income-based limits and your real obligations, not to a percentage.

long-term-vs-short-term-disability.title: Long-Term vs. Short-Term Disability Insurance: What High Earners Need to Know
long-term-vs-short-term-disability.description: Short-term covers months; long-term covers years. For high earners, individual long-term coverage fills the gap above the group cap and stays portable.
long-term-vs-short-term-disability.tldr: Short-term disability covers a brief recovery, usually a few months. Long-term disability is the protection that matters for a serious claim, and for high earners it has to be individual. Group long-term commonly caps around $10,000 on base salary, is taxable when employer-paid, and runs own-occupation for roughly 24 months before an any-occupation test. An individual long-term policy is indemnity, portable, and fills the long-term gap above the group cap.

mental-nervous-limitations.title: Mental and Nervous Limitations in Disability Insurance: Who Faces the 24-Month Cap
mental-nervous-limitations.description: Full-benefit-period mental health coverage is available across all five major disability carriers for most professions. Learn who faces the 24-month cap and how to avoid it.
mental-nervous-limitations.tldr: The 24-month mental and nervous cap is not universal. For most professions, full-benefit-period mental health coverage is available across all five major carriers, by default or by election. The cap is required only for a high-risk group: anesthesiology, emergency medicine, pain management, nurse anesthetists, and general dentistry. Because mental and nervous conditions are the single most common reason a policy comes back with an exclusion in our placed book, the real lever is applying before any mental-health history is on record.

multi-life-disability-insurance.title: Multi-Life and GSI Disability Insurance for Firms and Practices
multi-life-disability-insurance.description: Multi-life lets a firm's professionals buy individual own-occupation policies together at a discount. GSI adds limited or no medical underwriting. Both stay portable.
multi-life-disability-insurance.tldr: Multi-life is a way for a firm or practice to put its professionals on individual own-occupation policies through one coordinated arrangement, typically with a premium discount for the group. Guaranteed Standard Issue (GSI), which The Standard offers for qualifying employer-sponsored cases, goes further by limiting or removing medical underwriting. Both keep the contract quality and portability of individual coverage, so each person owns a policy that travels with them.

own-occupation-by-carrier-comparison.title: Own-Occupation Definitions Compared Across the Top Carriers
own-occupation-by-carrier-comparison.description: How Guardian, Principal, MassMutual, Ameritas, and The Standard actually define own-occupation. Which build it into the base contract, which use a rider, where occupation class limits availability, and how residual coverage compares.
own-occupation-by-carrier-comparison.tldr: All five major individual carriers can be written as true own-occupation for most professions. Guardian and Ameritas build it into the base definition; MassMutual and Principal deliver it through their own-occupation definition or rider; The Standard through a rider whose availability depends on occupation class. None of the contracts name a specialty; the definition plus your occupation class is what governs.

own-occupation-disability-insurance.title: Own-Occupation Disability Insurance: What It Means and Why It Matters
own-occupation-disability-insurance.description: Own-occupation is the single most important provision that decides whether a disability claim pays. What true own-occupation means, how it differs from modified and any-occupation definitions, and what to verify before you buy.
own-occupation-disability-insurance.tldr: A true own-occupation policy pays if you cannot perform your own occupation, even if you choose to work in another job and keep earning. A modified definition stops paying once you work elsewhere; any-occupation pays only if you cannot work at all. What decides a claim is the definition you bought, applied to the duties of your occupation at the time disability begins. The definition is locked at application, so the time to get it right is before you ever file a claim.

presumptive-disability.title: Presumptive Disability: When Full Benefits Begin Immediately
presumptive-disability.description: Presumptive disability pays the full benefit, usually with no elimination period, for loss of sight, hearing, speech, or use of limbs. It is broadly available across the major carriers.
presumptive-disability.tldr: Presumptive disability pays the full monthly benefit, usually with no elimination period, when you suffer a defined catastrophic loss: sight, hearing, speech, or use of limbs. It presumes total disability without the normal proof of income loss or occupational testing, and it is broadly available across the major carriers, generally built into the base contract or included at no extra cost.

residual-disability-benefits.title: Residual Disability Benefits: How Partial-Loss Coverage Actually Pays
residual-disability-benefits.description: Most disability claims are partial, not total. Learn how residual benefits pay on a 15-20% income loss, why partial is residual, and how the recovery benefit works.
residual-disability-benefits.tldr: Residual disability, also called partial disability, pays a proportional benefit when a covered illness or injury reduces your income, not only when you cannot work at all. Most disabilities are partial, so this is the provision most high earners actually use. Across the five major carriers we place, the trigger is a 15% income loss (20% at The Standard), all five pay roughly a 50% minimum early in a claim, all include a recovery benefit for the return-to-work ramp, and none require a prior period of total disability. Because an individual policy already replaces only about a third of a high earner's income, residual is the part of the contract that does the most work over a career.

retirement-protection-rider.title: Retirement Protection Rider: Who It Actually Helps
retirement-protection-rider.description: A retirement protection rider keeps retirement contributions going during a disability. It is context-dependent and works best for young, long-horizon buyers.
retirement-protection-rider.tldr: A retirement protection rider replaces the retirement savings you would lose during a disability, typically by paying a separate benefit into a trust that is invested for retirement and released at your normal retirement age. Its value is context-dependent and rises with time horizon, so it favors younger buyers, which fits our book: the median age at issue is 36. For late-career buyers there is little horizon left to protect.

return-of-premium-rider.title: Return of Premium Rider: The Opportunity-Cost Problem
return-of-premium-rider.description: A return-of-premium rider can add roughly 30-50% to your base premium. For most high earners the opportunity cost makes it rarely worth it.
return-of-premium-rider.tldr: A return-of-premium rider refunds a portion of premiums if you reach a set age without a claim, but it adds roughly 30 to 50% to your base premium to do it. For most high earners the opportunity cost is the catch: the same dollars invested elsewhere typically grow to more than the refund, and the refund disappears entirely if you do claim. We rarely place it.

student-loan-rider.title: Student Loan Rider: Protecting Education Debt During a Disability
student-loan-rider.description: A student loan rider pays your education debt during a disability, separate from your base benefit. Most useful for residents and early-career professionals.
student-loan-rider.tldr: A student loan rider pays a benefit toward your education debt during a disability, on top of your base benefit, so a long claim does not put your loans into default. It matters most for residents and early-career professionals carrying large balances, and it is a strong reason to apply early. MassMutual offers a 20% medical-resident and 10% dental-resident discount, which makes locking in coverage in training especially efficient.

veterinarian-disability-insurance-traps.title: Disability Insurance Traps for Veterinarians
veterinarian-disability-insurance-traps.description: Veterinarians are often placed in a lower occupation class than physicians, face real injury and zoonotic exposure, and need true own-occupation. Here are the traps to avoid.
veterinarian-disability-insurance-traps.tldr: Veterinarians face a set of disability traps that other professionals do not. They are often classed lower than physicians, which raises premium and can limit available riders and benefit. Their work carries genuine physical and zoonotic exposure, and their income often splits between veterinary services and practice ownership. The provision that decides a claim is a true own-occupation definition applied to your real duties, and association coverage alone rarely fills the gap. Terms are set at underwriting, so applying while healthy is the lever.

when-to-buy-disability-insurance.title: When to Buy Disability Insurance: Why Earlier Almost Always Wins
when-to-buy-disability-insurance.description: The median age at issue in our book is 36, and about 28% of policies get an exclusion or rating. Apply before health events to protect price and coverage.
when-to-buy-disability-insurance.tldr: The best time to buy disability insurance is while you are young and healthy, because age and health are the two largest levers on both price and coverage and both only move one direction over time. In our placed book the median age at issue is 36, and about 28 percent of policies come back with an exclusion or a rating. Applying before a health event is the most reliable way to keep coverage broad and the price low.

ameritas.title: Ameritas Disability Insurance Review: DInamic Cornerstone
ameritas.description: A provision-level review of Ameritas DInamic Cornerstone (form 4601NC): true own-occupation in the base definition, a residual rider that pays full loss for the first three months, a mental-health cap that excludes neurocognitive disease, the highest BOE limit of the majors, and the conservative underwriting that defines the carrier.
ameritas.tldr: Ameritas DInamic Cornerstone (form 4601NC) puts true own-occupation in the base definition, no rider required, and pairs it with strong early-claim residual mechanics (full loss paid for the first three months) and a mental-health limitation that notably excludes neurocognitive disease. Ameritas carries the highest business-overhead-expense limit of the majors ($100,000 a month). The trade-off is underwriting: in our experience it sits toward the conservative end, second only to Guardian, so it rewards clean, well-documented files.

guardian.title: Guardian Disability Insurance Review: Provider Choice Contract
guardian.description: A provision-level review of Guardian's Provider Choice (issued by Berkshire Life, form 18ID): true own-occupation definition options, the strongest mental-health coverage of the majors, residual at a 15% income-loss threshold, COLA variants, catastrophic riders, and who it fits.
guardian.tldr: Guardian's Provider Choice, issued by Berkshire Life (form 18ID), is, in our view, the strongest individual disability contract for high earners. It offers a true own-occupation definition for the full benefit period (with specialty and MD/DO-enhanced variants), and for most occupations places no time cap on mental and nervous claims, the strongest position of the five carriers we place (the higher-risk specialties, including anesthesiology, ER, pain management, and nurse anesthetists, are required to take the 24-month limitation, as they are everywhere). Its partial-disability rider triggers at a 15% income loss with no time-or-duties requirement. The trade-offs are higher premiums and more stringent underwriting: Guardian generally sits at the higher end on price and is, in our experience, the strictest of the five to move on a borderline file.

massmutual.title: MassMutual Disability Insurance Review: Radius Choice
massmutual.description: A provision-level review of MassMutual Radius Choice (form ICC15-XLIS-RC): true own-occupation via the Own Occupation Rider with billing-code specialty recognition, a 3% compound no-cap COLA, the mental-health endorsement that removes the 24-month cap, dividend potential, and the only major that writes active-duty military.
massmutual.tldr: MassMutual Radius Choice (form ICC15-XLIS-RC) is a participating, dividend-eligible contract from a mutual company. It delivers true own-occupation through its Own Occupation Rider, with specialty recognition tied to CPT/ADA billing codes, and carries a 3% compound COLA with no cap. It is one of the few carriers that can extend mental-health coverage past the 24-month cap (by endorsement, outside California), and, to our knowledge, the only major that will write a policy for active-duty military, with military, government, and resident discounts.

principal.title: Principal Disability Insurance Review: Income Protector
principal.description: A provision-level review of Principal's Income Protector (form ICC22-800): true own-occupation as placed, two no-cost benefit-growth riders, a no-cost presumptive rider with no elimination period, a generous residual benefit, and one of the deepest rider menus of the major carriers. Plus the underwriting flexibility we rely on.
principal.tldr: Principal Income Protector (form ICC22-800) is a top-tier individual disability contract on its own merits: true own-occupation as we place it, two no-cost growth riders (3% automatic annual increase plus a no-medical benefit-increase rider), a no-cost presumptive benefit with no elimination period, a residual rider that pays a 50% floor for the first year and treats a 75% loss as total, and a deep menu including catastrophic, lump-sum cancer/stroke, and business-protection riders. As a bonus, its underwriting is, in our experience, the most flexible of the five, which is what saves a complicated case.

the-standard.title: The Standard Disability Insurance Review: Platinum Advantage
the-standard.description: A provision-level review of The Standard's Platinum Advantage: a regular-occupation base with true own-occupation via a class-gated rider (3A/3P/3D and higher), three residual tiers at a 20% threshold, a 3%/6% COLA choice, a Family Care Benefit, and a strong student-loan rider. Where it fits and where occupation class decides.
the-standard.tldr: The Standard's Platinum Advantage is a clear, well-built contract whose true own-occupation comes through an Own Occupation Rider available only to occupation classes 3A/3P/3D and higher. The base definition is a regular-occupation definition. It offers three residual tiers (20% income-loss threshold), a choice of 3% or 6% COLA, an unusual Family Care Benefit, and a student-loan rider up to $2,500 a month. Occupation class, more than the carrier, decides what you can get.

aicpa-disability-plan.title: AICPA Disability Plan: Coverage Limits CPAs Should Know
aicpa-disability-plan.description: AICPA group disability has caps and definition limits that leave senior CPAs underinsured. See where it helps and where individual coverage matters.
aicpa-disability-plan.tldr: The AICPA group disability plan offers CPAs affordable baseline coverage but caps benefits below what partners and senior professionals earn. The plan uses broad accounting definitions rather than distinguishing between audit, tax, advisory, and forensic specialties. Individual supplemental coverage is essential for CPAs earning above plan caps, particularly those in partner-track or ownership roles.

aafp-disability-plan.title: AAFP Disability Plan Review: Where Coverage Falls Short
aafp-disability-plan.description: AAFP coverage caps at $8K-$12K monthly, far below mid-career family physician income. See where the plan fits and where individual coverage matters.
aafp-disability-plan.tldr: The AAFP group disability plan provides accessible coverage for family physicians but caps benefits significantly below what primary care physicians earn at peak practice. The plan uses broad medical professional definitions rather than distinguishing primary care duties from other medical work, lacks critical riders for residual disability and income growth, and ties coverage to AAFP membership. Family physicians earning above $220,000 should treat the AAFP plan as a supplement, not as primary income protection.

aao-disability-plan.title: AAO Disability Plan: Coverage Guide for Ophthalmologists
aao-disability-plan.description: AAO coverage doesn't track fine-motor and procedural risk specific to eye surgery. See where the plan fits and how individual coverage compares.
aao-disability-plan.tldr: The AAO group disability plan provides accessible coverage for ophthalmologists through streamlined enrollment but caps benefits well below what most eye surgeons earn. The plan uses broad medical practice language rather than distinguishing between surgical, laser, and non-procedural ophthalmology work, limiting its application when ophthalmologists face visual or motor impairment. Benefit caps, missing residual disability riders, and lack of COLA protection create substantial income gaps. Ophthalmologists earning above $250,000 should treat the AAO plan as supplemental coverage only, not primary income protection.

aap-disability-plan.title: AAP Disability Plan: Coverage Gaps Pediatricians Should Know
aap-disability-plan.description: AAP group disability caps benefits below mid-career pediatricians and uses generic medical language. Where the plan fits and where individual matters.
aap-disability-plan.tldr: The AAP group disability plan offers pediatricians accessible baseline coverage but caps benefits well below what pediatricians earn during peak practice years. The plan uses broad medical professional definitions rather than distinguishing pediatric practice from other specialties, lacks critical riders for partial disability and future increases, and ties coverage to AAP membership. Pediatricians with earnings above $200,000, particularly those in subspecialties or practice ownership, should treat the AAP plan as a supplement, not as primary protection.

acep-disability-plan.title: ACEP Disability Plan Review: Gaps ER Physicians Should Know
acep-disability-plan.description: ACEP group disability caps benefits below ER physician income and uses generic medical language. Where it fits and where individual coverage matters.
acep-disability-plan.tldr: The ACEP group disability plan provides accessible coverage for emergency physicians but caps benefits significantly below what EM physicians earn in high-performing shifts and contracts. The plan uses broad medical professional definitions rather than addressing the unique demands of emergency medicine work, lacks critical riders for partial disability and income growth, and ties coverage to ACEP membership. Emergency physicians earning above $300,000, particularly those with shift-based income documentation or multiple income sources, should treat the ACEP plan as a supplement, not as primary protection.

acs-disability-plan.title: ACS Disability Plan: Coverage Gaps Surgeons Should Know
acs-disability-plan.description: ACS group coverage uses general medical language that won't protect surgical-specific procedures. Where the plan fits and where it leaves income exposed.
acs-disability-plan.tldr: The ACS group disability plan provides accessible coverage for surgeons through simplified enrollment but caps benefits significantly below what most surgeons earn. The plan uses broad surgical occupational language rather than distinguishing between different types of surgical work, limiting its application when surgeons face partial functional impairment. Benefit caps, missing residual disability riders, and lack of COLA protection create substantial income gaps. Surgeons earning above $200,000 should treat the ACS plan as supplemental coverage only, not primary income protection.

ada-disability-plan.title: ADA Disability Plan Review: Gaps Dentists Should Know
ada-disability-plan.description: ADA coverage has procedural gaps that miss dental-specific disability. See what the plan covers, what it leaves out, and when individual coverage matters.
ada-disability-plan.tldr: The ADA group disability plan gives dentists affordable baseline coverage but caps benefits below what most practice owners and specialists earn. The plan's occupational definition may not adequately distinguish between procedural dentistry and non-clinical work. Dentists face elevated disability risk from musculoskeletal injuries, hand tremor, and repetitive strain, making specialty-specific individual coverage essential.

ama-group-disability.title: AMA Disability Insurance Review: Plan Limits for Physicians
ama-group-disability.description: AMA group disability uses generic occupational language that misses specialty risk. See where the plan fits and how it compares to individual coverage.
ama-group-disability.tldr: The AMA group disability plan provides accessible coverage for physicians through group purchasing but caps benefits well below what high-earning and specialist physicians actually earn. The plan uses broad occupational language tied to general medical practice rather than specialty-specific criteria, lacks essential riders for partial disability and inflation protection, and ties coverage to AMA membership status. Physicians earning above $200,000 should treat the AMA plan as supplemental coverage, not primary income protection.

avma-disability-plan.title: AVMA Disability Plan Review: Gaps Veterinarians Should Know
avma-disability-plan.description: AVMA group coverage misses surgical risk and caps benefits below mid-career vet income. See where the plan fits and where individual coverage matters.
avma-disability-plan.tldr: The AVMA group disability plan provides veterinarians with accessible baseline coverage but caps benefits below what practice owners, specialists, and experienced practitioners earn. The plan uses broad veterinary definitions that do not distinguish between surgical, large-animal, and companion-animal practice. Veterinarians face elevated disability risk from animal-related injuries, musculoskeletal strain, and occupational exposure, making specialty-specific individual coverage essential.

carrierMatrix.ownOcc: True own-occupation in the base definition (Enhanced True Own Occupation); issued by Berkshire Life, form 18ID.
strengths: No default mental/nervous cap for most occupations (the cap is required for high-risk groups including CRNAs)
carrierMatrix.bestFor: High earners who prioritize contract strength and claims reputation

carrierMatrix.ownOcc: True own-occupation via the Own Occupation Rider.
carrierMatrix.bestFor: Long-term holders, active military, government employees, and residents

strengths: Deep rider menu and no-cost growth riders
Most flexible underwriting of the five
Strong for complex or borderline files
carrierMatrix.bestFor: Complex or borderline files; surgical and dental specialties

carrierMatrix.ownOcc: True own-occupation in the base definition.
strengths: Highest business-overhead-expense limit of the majors ($100K)
carrierMatrix.bestFor: Clean files; owners needing high business-overhead limits

carrierMatrix.ownOcc: True own-occupation via the Own Occupation Rider, available to higher occupation classes (3A/3P/3D+); cannot be written true own-occ for a CRNA at class 2P.
carrierMatrix.bestFor: Higher occupation classes seeking contract clarity

riders.name: Residual (Partial) Disability
riders.summary: Pays a proportional benefit when a covered disability reduces income by roughly 15-20% or more. Most disabilities are partial, not total; without this rider a policy only pays when you cannot work at all.

riders.name: Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA)
riders.summary: Increases the monthly benefit during long claims to keep pace with inflation. Compound 3% is the standard structure. Value drops materially past age 50 because the remaining benefit horizon shortens.

riders.name: Future Increase Option (FIO)
riders.summary: Locks in insurability. Lets the insured buy more coverage as income rises without new medical underwriting. Typically available until age 55 or a set number of years.

riders.name: Own-Occupation Enhancement
riders.audience: anyone with a modified-own-occ base policy
riders.summary: Extends the true own-occupation definition if the base contract is modified own-occ. Not needed when the base policy is already true own-occ to age 65.

riders.name: Retirement Protection
riders.audience: high earners with significant retirement-savings goals
riders.summary: Continues retirement-fund contributions during a disability. Valuable with a long career horizon and large retirement-savings targets; less valuable for late-career buyers.

riders.name: Return of Premium
riders.summary: Adds roughly 30-50% to the base premium. The opportunity cost of that extra premium invested elsewhere almost always exceeds the returned amount.

riders.name: Catastrophic Disability
riders.summary: Adds benefit for severe disabilities (loss of two or more activities of daily living). Coverage is meaningful but catastrophic disabilities are rare; cost-to-expected-value is typically unfavorable.

riders.name: Social Insurance Supplement
riders.summary: Pays if a Social Security disability claim is denied. Most high earners will not qualify for SSDI under the any-occupation standard, so the trigger rarely fires usefully.
